why: Lay medication wording should map water pill to the diuretic drug class, not literal water or hydrotherapy.
When the lay phrase and drug name appear together, furosemide should lead and literal water concepts should stay out of the visible result set.
Everyday wording for a urine test should retrieve urinalysis rather than returning no clinical result.
Clinical synonym control for the pee-test row; urinalysis should stay directly retrievable.
Lay abdominal-pain wording should retrieve abdominal pain before generic pain or belly anatomy.
Clinical synonym control for the belly-pain row; abdominal pain should stay directly retrievable.
Lay diabetes wording should map to diabetes mellitus rather than sugar chemicals or a literal sugar-diabetes label.
Lay ECG wording should retrieve electrocardiography, not heart anatomy, trachea, or TRAC gene noise.
Abbreviated patient wording should retrieve electrocardiography rather than only an ECG-finding concept.
Lay colonoscopy wording should retrieve colonoscopy, not colon anatomy or unrelated colon devices.
Lay upper-endoscopy wording should retrieve upper GI endoscopy/EGD, not stomach anatomy.
Lay anticoagulant wording should retrieve anticoagulants rather than returning no clinical result.
Clinical synonym control for the blood-thinner row; anticoagulants should stay directly retrievable.
